EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and fast-moving system moved across the northern Pacific and into Nevada on the 26th. The system brought a relatively quick period of heavy snow to the Lake Tahoe area, with some snow spreading into the western Great Basin from the afternoon of the 25th through the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 12 to 18 inches of snow fell with this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and fast-moving system moved across the northern Pacific and into Nevada on the 26th. The system brought a relatively quick period of heavy snow to the Lake Tahoe area, with some snow spreading into the western Great Basin from the afternoon of the 25th through the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 1 to 3 inches of snow fell with this system. However, some areas in the foothills and between Washoe Valley and Carson City received 4 to 7 inches of snow. Some of the heavier snow totals may have been enhanced by lake effect snow off of Lake Tahoe, especially in the early morning hours of the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modest tap of mid-latitude moisture extending across the Pacific brought significant precipitation to the northern Sierra from the 20th through the 23rd. Snowpack in the northern Sierra rose from 5 to 10 percent of normal on the 20th to around 40 percent of normal by the 23rd. Snow levels were relatively high for the periods of heaviest precipitation so the most significant snowfall remained mainly above 6500-7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain below 7500 feet on the evening of the 20th, therefore the heaviest snowfall remained at the higher elevations. Snowfall totals include 21 inches at the Mount Rose Ski area and 12 inches near Incline Village but only 4 to 6 inches at the Diamond Peak Ski area and 2 inches in Glenbrook. Overall liquid precipitation totals of 1.5 to 3 inches fell in this very wet storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and cold low pressure moved from the Gulf of Alaska on the 28th to the West Coast on the 29th bringing snow and gusty winds to the Sierra and western Nevada. Unsettled conditions continued into March 1 as the cold trough slowly moved through  the Western CONUS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts of 59 to 71 mph occurred on the 29th in and south of the Mason and Smith Valleys, with the highest winds over Sweetwater (hwy 338) and Anchorite (hwy 359) Summits. However, the highest winds in the area occurred along hwy 95 near Walker Lake where winds gusted between 75 and 89 mph between 0800PST and 1100PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought locally high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 2 miles SE of Mt. Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two Pacific storm systems brought strong winds to the Mojave Desert, followed by rain and snow. Light snow fell at the lower elevations but did not accumulate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell for five minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough dropped along the Pacific Northwest coast on the 8th and into northern California and the interior Northwest on the 9th. The trough brought a narrow band of heavy lake effect snow to portions of western Nevada to the east of Lake Tahoe on the 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 7 inches (locally to 9) of snow fell in a narrow lake effect snow band extending from Lake Tahoe to near or just west-northwest of Carson City. Lesser amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell between Carson City and Minden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from November 29th through December 2nd, bringing high elevation snowfall, heavy lower elevation rainfall, and strong winds to western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 35 and 45 mph on the 29th into the morning of the 30th. However, locally higher gusts were reported including 53 in Smith Valley and between 71 and 88 mph at the Walker Lake RAWS near Hawthorne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from November 29th through December 2nd, bringing high elevation snowfall, heavy lower elevation rainfall, and strong winds to western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow levels remained fairly high during this event with only up to 4 inches down to 6800 feet. However, at Daggett Pass (7334 feet) 8 inches was reported with amounts of 17 to 23 inches above 8000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from November 29th through December 2nd, bringing high elevation snowfall, heavy lower elevation rainfall, and strong winds to western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 68 and 90 mph in the foothills with the strongest winds in the Galena area where a 20-second sustained wind speed of 90 mph was reported by a spotter. Over Virginia Peak (SW of Pyramid Lake), winds gusted as high as 127 mph. In the Reno area, winds gusted to 67 mph at the National Weather Service office, 62 mph in West Reno, and 59 mph at the Reno-Tahoe airport. Damage to a hangar at the Reno airport was noted around 0300PST on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at the Desert National Wildlife Refuge RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning damaged a cell phone tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nipton Road was closed west of Searchlight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three feet of standing water covered Buffalo near Grand Teton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and rocks were over El Capitan Way between Gilmore and Alexander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought locally high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 40 miles E of Beatty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One swift water rescue was performed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Dam Road was washed out near Highway 93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of Highway 93 was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris washed over Highway 375 near Reveille Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter fell in a swath from Summerlin to North Las Vegas.  According to a local body shop, ten thousand insurance claims were filed for hail damage to vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra Nevada and Spring Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sites on the east slopes of the Spring Mountains reported high winds. The peak gust occurred 2 miles SE of Mount Charleston at 1150 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought isolated heavy snow to the mountains of southern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen inches of snow fell at the Las Vegas Ski and Snowboard Resort.

CZ_NAME: LINCOLN COUNTY EXCEPT THE SHEEP RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought isolated heavy snow to the southern Great Basin and isolated high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in Pioche in just three and a half hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system and cold front brought scattered high winds to the Mojave Desert and Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These gusts occurred at the Desert Rock Airport (KDRA).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system and cold front brought scattered high winds to the Mojave Desert and Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 9 miles N of Mercury.

CZ_NAME: LINCOLN COUNTY EXCEPT THE SHEEP RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system and cold front brought scattered high winds to the Mojave Desert and Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 1 mile WNW of Rachel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system and cold front brought scattered high winds to the Mojave Desert and Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 40 miles E of Beatty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated a 60 mph wind gust from the same thunderstorm which produced large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water came up over the sidewalks and into some businesses along Main Street in Pioche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skydiver died when shifting winds caused his parachute to fail. At least one media source reported the event as a dust devil, but this is doubtful.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skydiver was killed at the Jean Dry Lake Bed when shifting winds caused his parachute to lose lift. At least one media source reported the event as a dust devil, but this is doubtful.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure system brought widespread high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust during this event occurred at the Mercury-Desert Rock Airport (KDRA) at 0925 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open residential burn the previous weekend re-ignited as winds gusted to 40-45 mph on the afternoon of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An open residential burn in the Topaz Ranch Estates (a couple miles north of Topaz Lake) the previous weekend re-ignited as winds gusted to 40-45 mph on the afternoon of the 22nd. The fire claimed several cars, as well as 19 structures including 2 homes. The total acreage of the fire was 7152. Fire fighting costs (not fire damage) reached more than 3 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved across the North Pacific and into the West by early on April 1st bringing strong winds to the Sierra and western Nevada on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds on the 31st reached 40-47 mph with gusts 65-70 mph from Anchorite Summit (hwy 359) to Yerington with gusts as high as 69 mph along hwy 95 near Schurz. The Walker Lake NDOT sensor had a 76 mph gust at 1330PST. NDOT reported a dust storm which closed hwy 95 from Hawthorne to Luning late in the afternoon. Finally, the Yerington sheriff's office reported power lines and 2 telephone poles down, 3 transformers on fire, and the entire town without power at 1530PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above average to record high temperatures, as well as an upper low on the 23rd, brought strong thunderstorms to portions of western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust was measured at the Bluewing Mt RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above average to record high temperatures, as well as an upper low on the 23rd, brought strong thunderstorms to portions of western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Naval Air Station in Fallon reported this wind gust. Sustained winds were 41 mph at 1452PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved across the North Pacific and into the West by early on April 1st bringing strong winds to the Sierra and western Nevada on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread gusts of 35-45 mph were reported with this system. However, winds were locally higher with sustained winds of 40-50 mph (for one hour) on Lake Tahoe and local gusts up to 65 mph (up to 76 over ridges) in the Carson Range. Winds up to 4 feet were noted on Lake Tahoe on the north shore of Lake Tahoe in California.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 59 mph at the Morey Creek RAWS and downed trees in Paradise Valley.
Temperatures reached Excessive Heat Warning criteria in Las Vegas for three consecutive days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One man died of heat complications.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a total of 16 fires in northwest Arizona and southern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started two fires in the Pahrump area.  One burned a tree, and the other burned 10-20 acres of brush northeast of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from off the California coast into western Nevada between the 4th and the 5th. This brought high winds to portions of western Nevada on the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 41 and 53 mph with gust up to 64 mph were reported at the Schurz NDOT site and Hawthorne AWOS. In addition, a few trees and power lines were downed in Yerington along with visibility down to 100 yds.  In Hawthorne, visibility was down to 1/2 mile at the airport. Fourteen miles south of Walker Lake along highway 95, low visibility was deemed responsible for a 3 car pile and the closure of the highway between Fallon and Luning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist flow of air off the Pacific Ocean overrunning cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Elko county produced heavy snowfall. Wild Horse Reservoir reported 15 inches of snow and Owyhee 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture brought heavy snow to portions of northern Elko County. Wild Horse Reservoir reported 15 inches of snow, Owyhee 11 inches, and 7 inches in Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from off the Oregon and Washington coast early on the 19th to the northern Rockies early on the 20th. This brought strong winds to the Reno area on the 19th, which helped fan a large wildfire just south of Reno.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 60 and 84 mph for areas along and west of highway 395 from Reno south into the Carson Valley on the 19th. The highest winds were in and near the foothills south of Reno with 78 mph at the Galena RAWS and 84 mph at the Washoe Valley NDOT sensor. Strong winds were responsible for a blown over tractor trailer along highway 395 near Red Rock Road (north valleys of Reno) which caused 3 injuries, damage to 13 vehicles, and the shutting down of highway 395.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist flow off of the Pacific brought strong winds, heavy rain, and high elevation snow (mostly above 7500 feet) in the Sierra from the 1st through the 3rd. The heavy rain brought areas of minor flooding near the Truckee River and for some secondary roads in the Reno, Carson City, and Dayton areas on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 60 and 80 mph in and near Hawthorne on the 2nd, with up to 60 mph in the Mason Valley and up to 72 mph at the Brawley Peak RAWS. Winds caused power outages and roof damage in Yerington with damage to a facade and a street sign blown down in Hawthorne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist flow off of the Pacific brought strong winds, heavy rain, and high elevation snow (mostly above 7500 feet) in the Sierra from the 1st through the 3rd. The heavy rain brought areas of minor flooding near the Truckee River and for some secondary roads in the Reno, Carson City, and Dayton areas on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 60 and 69 mph west of Fallon and Lovelock on the 2nd, including Stagecoach, Silver Springs, and Pyramid Lake. The largest impact was noted in Nixon, where a spotter noted shingles off rooves, a trailer overturned, a greenhouse and fences blown down, and tree branches broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist flow off of the Pacific brought strong winds, heavy rain, and high elevation snow (mostly above 7500 feet) in the Sierra from the 1st through the 3rd. The heavy rain brought areas of minor flooding near the Truckee River and for some secondary roads in the Reno, Carson City, and Dayton areas on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley wind gusts between 60 and 70 mph were noted from the morning of the 1st into the morning of the 2nd, with the most widespread coverage early on the 2nd. In the foothills, wind gusts were between 70 and 88 mph. At Virginia Peak, winds gusted to 140 mph. Damage reports included downed fences and power lines in southwest Reno and in the Minden-Gardnerville area, several trucks overturned in Washoe Valley, and damage to a transmitter at KOLO (Reno) which knocked a broadcast off the air. Finally, high winds caused the cancellation of a number of flights at the Reno-Tahoe airport during the afternoon and evening of 12/1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from the 21st through early on the 24th bringing heavy snow to the Sierra along with snow and strong wind for far western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 60 and 77 mph during the afternoon of the 21st, with the highest winds in the foothills west of highway 395. These winds caused several tractor trailer trucks to be blown over on highway 395 between the Stead and Red Rock exits (North Valleys of Reno). In addition, at least 3 planes were diverted from the Reno-Tahoe airport with sustained winds over 40 mph between 1200PST and 1300PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped south through eastern California on the 13th, bringing a persistent band of snow to Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell between Hawthorne and Luning with little or no snow north and west of Hawthorne. However, from Luning to Mina (and likely across much of eastern Mineral County), 4 to 7 inches of snow fell from the evening of the 12th through mid morning on the 13th. The snow caused school transportation in Mineral County to be suspended on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from the 21st through early on the 24th bringing heavy snow to the Sierra along with snow and strong wind for far western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and a half to 2.5 feet of snow fell south of highway 50 with 2.5 to 4 feet (locally 4 to 5 feet) north of highway 50 between the afternoon of the 21st and late evening on the 23rd. In addition to heavy snow, winds gusted peridiodically to between 30 and 40 mph bringing restricted visibility at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from the 21st through early on the 24th bringing heavy snow to the Sierra along with snow and strong wind for far western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large range of snowfall was reported on the 22nd and 23rd. For the lower valleys along the highway 395 corridor, 1 to 3 inches of snowfall was common. However, in the foothills 6 to 12 inches with local amounts as high as 15 to 19 inches were noted. Some notable totals include 7.5 inches in Cold Springs, 15 inches 2 miles west of Verdi, and 16 inches 5 miles southwest of New Washoe City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado was photographed just east of Carlin. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was photographed looking west towards Carlin. The exact location is an estimate based on conversations with the person who took the photograph. He said it occurred sometime between 4 pm-5 pm. The EF0 designation was also an estimate as no damage was reported or found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought a few hours of high winds to portions of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak measured gust during this event was 65 mph 13 miles NW of Mercury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and cold low pressure moved from the Gulf of Alaska on the 28th to the West Coast on the 29th bringing snow and gusty winds to the Sierra and western Nevada. Unsettled conditions continued into March 1 as the cold trough slowly moved through  the Western CONUS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 20 inches of snow fell with this system from Incline Village to Mount Rose, with considerably less snowfall from Marlette Lake south (up to 8 inches). In addition to snowfall, gusty winds of 35-45 mph occurred on the 29th with local gusts 58-62 mph from Glenbrook Canyon to Little Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wrap-around precipitation from low pressure moving through south-central California brought a modest snowfall to the Lake Tahoe area on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches fell with this system, with local amounts of 12 to 17 inches near the Mount Rose and Sky Tavern ski areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across northern and central Nevada brought wind gusts as high as 83 mph which caused some structural damage and blew several vehicles off the highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 83 mph were observed by the Morey Creek RAWS, 77 mph at the Double H RAWS, and 72 mph at the Texas Springs RAWS. At Paradise Hills, not far from Morey Creek, a Ham Radio Operator lost an estimated three to four thousand dollars in damage to towers, antennas, feed lines, and house roofing and ventilation.||These strong winds also blew a couple of vehicles off area roadways. A Semi truck traveling westbound on Interstate 80 10 miles west of Golconda Summit was blown over by strong winds. A pickup truck hauling a 33 foot trailer on U.S. highway 95 north of Orovada was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving through the Pacific Northwest brought strong, gusty winds to western Nevada on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 55 mph were recorded on the 13th. However, localized areas saw stronger winds, including the Reno-Tahoe airport with sustained winds of 40-44 mph with gusts up to 59 mph, Galena RAWS with gusts 60-70 mph (as high as 80 mph 0800-0900PST), and Washoe Valley with gusts up to 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska on the 16th and 17th and brought heavy snow to the Sierra and strong, gusty winds to portions of western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen to 24 inches of snow fell near Diamond Peak ski area with 22 to 32 inches from Incline Village (above 7000-7500 ft) to the Mount Rose Ski area. Below 7000 feet, most snow fell from the evening of the 16th through the early morning of the 17th including 10 inches at Glenbrook and 12 inches at Incline Village (at 6700 ft).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska on the 16th and 17th and brought heavy snow to the Sierra and strong, gusty winds to portions of western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred with this system. However, much stronger local wind gusts occurred between 2100PST on the 16th through 0100PST on the 17th, including gusts over 60 mph (briefly 74-95 mph between 2300-2400PST) at the Walker Lake NDOT sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from off the Oregon and Washington coast early on the 19th to the northern Rockies early on the 20th. This brought strong winds to the Reno area on the 19th, which helped fan a large wildfire just south of Reno.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds (up to 84 mph) and the improper disposal of fireplace ashes caused the rapid spread of a fire originating at the north end of Washoe Valley early in the afternoon on the 19th. Thousands of people were forced to evacuate from the swift-moving fire. Around 3100 acres were burned with 28 homes destroyed (7 damaged) and one fatality attributed to the fire. ||For information purposes only (not direct costs from wildfire), the tally of fire-fighting related costs were at least 1.5 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved across the North Pacific and into the West by early on April 1st bringing strong winds to the Sierra and western Nevada on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds over 40 mph with gusts 58-64 mph were reported at the Reno-Tahoe airport, the National Weather Service in Reno, and in Palomino Valley (southwest of Pyramid Lake). Gusts to 66 mph occurred at the Reno-Stead airport with gusts over 60 mph along highway 395 in the Carson and Washoe Valleys. Winds over Virginia Peak gusted to 106 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 66 mph at the Ruby Valley RAWS site in the Ruby Mountains.
A very strong cold front brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Isolated heavy snow also fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A porch was torn off a home in Beatty, and a wind observing tower 17 miles ESE of Beatty was blown down after registering a 78 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved across the North Pacific and into the West by early on April 1st bringing strong winds to the Sierra and western Nevada on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds on the 31st were sustained between 35 and 47 mph with gusts 60-64 mph at the Fallon and Lovelock ASOS sites and at the Humboldt House NDOT site. Elsewhere, widespread gusts over 50 mph (and 61 mph at the Silver Springs AWOS) occurred on the 31st. Strong winds caused blowing dust which reduced visibility to 1/4 mile or less at times in Lovelock and Fallon. In addition, a window was reported broken by the wind (not debris) in Lovelock and a single-wide trailer was blown over near Rye Patch Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed 16 power poles along U.S. highway 95 about 15 miles north of Orovada. The downed wires then started a fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed 16 power poles and the downed wires started a fire. The wind gust speed is estimated based on the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with lingering monsoon moisture to produce flash flooding and severe weather over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water and debris were over Highway 159.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with lingering monsoon moisture to produce flash flooding and severe weather over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris were crossing Arroyo Road in Blue Diamond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with lingering monsoon moisture to produce flash flooding and severe weather over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail was reported by several sources as it tracked from Anthem to East Las Vegas. One car windshield was cracked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with lingering monsoon moisture to produce flash flooding and severe weather over the Mojave Desert.
A strong low pressure system combined with lingering monsoon moisture to produce flash flooding and severe weather over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding affected much of the Las Vegas Valley. Numerous streets were flooded, and at least one freeway on ramp was closed. Water entered one home, and there were at least four swift water rescues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated early morning thunderstorms rumbled over Henderson.  One lightning strike caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike knocked out power to 1000 customers, and started a fire which destroyed a vacant office building at Eastern and Wigwam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  A few storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down large tree limbs in and just south of Beatty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  A few storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Avenue in Beatty was washed out at the west end, and covered with mud elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  A few storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud washed onto several roads in the Nevada Treasure RV Resort in Pahrump, and there were several inches of water and mud on Highway 160 just north of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  A few storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud washed onto portions of Ash Meadows Road near Amargosa Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with lingering monsoon moisture to produce flash flooding and severe weather over the Mojave Desert.
A strong low pressure system combined with lingering monsoon moisture to produce flash flooding and severe weather over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The main road to Spring Mountain Ranch State Park flooded near the park entrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 169 was closed near Whitmore Street due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in Sandy Valley were flooded with water and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust devil in North Las Vegas damaged a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil hit a home near Grand Teton and Aliante Parkway, taking 20 to 30 tiles off the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached in Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperature at McCarran Airport (KLAS) reached 112 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four mile section of Highway 266 near Lida Summit was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected much of the Las Vegas Valley. Numerous roads were closed, and several people were rescued from cars. A church was damaged by three feet of water when a retaining wall collapsed. Several people were rescued from washes. One man died when he fell into the Pittman Wash and was swept downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of water ran down a road in Sloan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding affected much of Valley of Fire State Park. Mud and debris flowed over roads in at least ten places in the park. White Dome Road was undercut and washed out. In addition, Exit 75 off Interstate 15 was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water were running across Northshore Road near mile marker 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple streets in Boulder City were closed due to water and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from off the California coast into western Nevada between the 4th and the 5th. This brought high winds to portions of western Nevada on the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver Springs AWOS and Stagecoach NDOT site indicated winds sustained between 40 and 55 mph with gusts 66 to 69 mph on the afternoon of the 4th. Dead Camel RAWS (18 miles SW of Fallon) had winds sustained up to 42 mph with gusts to 60 mph. Several trees were down on roads with a total of 16-20 trees downed around Fernley. Also, some fences were downed and power out along with visibility down to 1/2 mile in Fernley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled scattered thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Isolated flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Rock Scenic Loop was flooded and closed. One vehicle was nearly swept away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture continued to fuel scattered thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Isolated severe weather and flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water flowed over both Bonnie Springs Road and the Spring Mountain Ranch State Park access road just west of Highway 159.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three people died of heat complications in Las Vegas during a particularly hot period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three people in the Las Vegas Valley died of heat complications.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two more people in the Las Vegas Valley died of heat complications at the end of a particularly hot period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two more people in the Las Vegas Valley died of heat complications.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture began to return from the southeast, fueling strong thunderstorms over the eastern Mojave Desert. Several storms produced severe weather and/or flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of monsoon moisture led to an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A low pressure system further enhanced the storms on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 59 mph at the North Spring Valley DRI mesonet site.
Heavy rainfall from a thunderstorm caused flash flooding in Ely. Law enforement reported water and debris running down several streets.
Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding near Kingston. Four to six inches of water was reported flowing across state highway 376 at milepost 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms resulted in a flash flood near Kingston with 4 to 6 inches of water reported flowing across state highway 376 at milepost 27. No reports of damages were received so monetary value is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot (near record) temperatures helped isolated thunderstorms produce strong outflow winds in Churchill County during the evening of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sensor west of Ragtown near highway 50 reported gusts to 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot (near record) temperatures helped isolated thunderstorms produce strong outflow winds in Churchill County during the evening of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused a 4 to 5 foot diameter tree to fall on St. Clair Street in Fallon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms caused heavy rainfall in the Pine Nut Range southeast of Gardnerville. The heavy rain fell on the Ray May wildfire burn scar which produced a large debris flow over highway 395.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large rocks and mud covered highway 395 near the Ray May burn scar as heavy rain caused the hillside to give way. The debris flow/rock slide caused highway 395 to be closed as heavy equipment was required to remove the rocks from the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system brought isolated high winds to the Mojave Desert and Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 40 miles E of Beatty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system brought isolated high winds to the Mojave Desert and Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 1 mile ESE of Red Rock Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system interacted with deep monsoon moisture to produce widespread thunderstorms and flash flooding. A few storms also produced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding affected much of the Las Vegas Valley. Numerous roads were closed due to water, mud, and rocks; and several people had to be rescued from vehicles. Many vehicles in parking lots were submerged in muddy water. Many homes, condos, and businesses flooded, and three dogs drowned in one home. Water got into buildings on the UNLV campus as well as a middle school and the MGM Grand Garden Arena. One landscaper died when flood waters swept him off his front-end loader.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving across southern Oregon and southern Idaho caused strong south winds across portions of northern Nevada. Wind gusts up to 86 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was observed at the Double H RAWS, 60 mph at the Morey Creek RAWS, and 58 mph at the Texas Springs RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving across southern Oregon and southern Idaho caused strong south winds across portions of northern Nevada. Wind gusts up to 86 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 66 mph was recorded at the Wells/Moor Grade mesonet site, 63 mph at the Pequop Summit mesonet site, and 59 mph at the Clover Valley mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving across southern Oregon and southern Idaho caused strong south winds across portions of northern Nevada. Wind gusts up to 86 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Down slope wind gusts to 86 mph were recorded at the Ruby Valley RAWS site on the East slopes of the Ruby Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 10 to 20 inches of snow fell on the 22nd and early on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture (precipitable water near 1 inch) moving north from southern California and Nevada combined with daytime heating to produce afternoon and evening thunderstorms with heavy rain on the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Reno news station meteorologist and a couple spotters sent several videos of fast flowing water over Nye Road near its intersection with Johnson Lane. The water caused severe damage to Nye Road. The water was also seen flowing down Johnson Lane closer to highway 395. The Douglas County Sheriff reported water flowing over multiple roads in the Minden-Johnson Lane area around 1720PST.
